title: Tokyo weekend — the quick brief
body: While the other two are at GO Fest + MSI in Daejeon, you've got a solo Tokyo weekend — Sat Jul 11 → Mon Jul 13, flying Jeju Air, Incheon (ICN) ↔ Tokyo–Narita (NRT). No time change — Korea and Japan are both UTC+9. Carry-on only: pack light, keep your passport on you. This is a crash-course — hit a few icons, eat well, grab snacks, and be back in Seoul to meet the guys Monday night ≈20:30. Checked Jul 2026; ⚠ re-verify flight times, ticket prices and hours before you fly.
checklist: The one hard deadline: back in central Seoul by ≈20:30 Mon Jul 13. Book a Narita→Incheon flight landing ICN by ≈18:00–18:30, then AREX/limo (~1 hr) into the city. ⚠ Don't cut it closer — Monday traffic + immigration eat time.
Top 3 if you do nothing else: Shibuya Crossing + Shibuya Sky at sunset · Senso-ji temple in Asakusa · a night out in Shinjuku (Omoide Yokocho / Golden Gai).
Book online now: Shibuya Sky (timed entry — sunset slots sell out) and teamLab Planets (15-min slots, weekend = busy). Grab both before you fly.
Pay with your phone: add Suica to Apple Pay (or buy a Welcome Suica, ≈¥1,500, at the airport) — tap for every train and most konbini.
Cash: cards work widely now, but carry ≈¥10,000 for small ramen shops, shrines and markets. No tipping in Japan.
Speed through arrival: pre-register Visit Japan Web (immigration + customs QR) before you land — official site; show the QR live from your phone, not a screenshot.

Korea re-entry Monday is a NEW entry: the same rules from Entry & documents (Plan tab) apply — US citizens have the K-ETA waived through Dec 31 2026, and you'll clear Korean immigration again. Have your docs ready both directions.

title: Flights, getting in & getting around
body: Jeju Air (7C) flies Incheon Terminal 1 → Tokyo–Narita (NRT), ~2.5 hr, no time change. Book at jejuair.net. ⚠ Confirm your exact times, terminal and baggage (a carry-on-only fare is cheapest) on your own booking — and pick a Monday return that lands ICN by ≈18:00–18:30 so you make Seoul by 20:30.
checklist: Narita → Tokyo, two easy trains. Narita Express (N'EX) runs straight to Shibuya / Shinjuku, ~65 min, ≈¥3,070–4,370 — best if you base in Shibuya. Keisei Skyliner hits Ueno / Nippori in ~41 min, ≈¥2,580 (≈¥2,310 booked online) — faster and cheaper but east-side. Skyliner official.

IC card: add Suica to Apple Pay before you land (easiest, carry-on-friendly), or buy a Welcome Suica (≈¥1,500, 28-day) at the JR East Travel Service Center at Narita. Tap for trains, konbini and vending machines.
Base yourself in Shibuya or Shinjuku — both sit on the JR Yamanote loop, within walking distance of nightlife and one train from most sights. A capsule or business hotel is plenty for two nights.
Coin lockers are everywhere (stations, ≈¥400–700) — stash your carry-on Saturday so you're not dragging it around before check-in.
Last trains run ~00:00–01:00. Miss it and it's a pricey taxi — check the last-train time on Google Maps (which does work in Japan, unlike Korea).
Etiquette: stand left on Tokyo escalators, keep phone calls off trains, and carry your trash until you find a bin — they're scarce.

Rough all-in budget: ~$450–650 for the weekend — Jeju Air round trip + 2 nights + Suica/trains + food + a couple of paid views (Shibuya Sky, teamLab). ⚠ Flights swing it most; this is your own line, separate from the Korea budget tab.

title: Your 3 days, roughly
body:
Sat Jul 11 — land & Shibuya night. Fly ICN→NRT (aim for a morning departure), train into Shibuya, lock the bag in a coin locker. Do the Shibuya Crossing at street level, then Shibuya Sky for sunset (pre-booked). Dinner in Shibuya, then wander Shinjuku — Omoide Yokocho yakitori + Golden Gai. Early-ish night; Sunday's the big one.

Sun Jul 12 — the full day. Morning at Senso-ji in Asakusa (go early, before the crowds; Nakamise street for snacks). Midday Meiji Jingu + Harajuku / Omotesando. Afternoon or evening teamLab Planets in Toyosu (booked slot). Into gaming? Swap in Akihabara for arcades and anime/game shops — right up your crew's alley.

Mon Jul 13 — light morning, then fly back. Keep it loose — it's a travel day. One easy thing near your route, a fast souvenir run (Donki or a station depachika), then head to Narita by early afternoon. Fly NRT→ICN, ride into Seoul, and meet the guys ≈20:30. ⚠ Give yourself buffer — don't schedule anything you can't drop.

title: Must-hit Tokyo
items: Shibuya Crossing — the world's busiest scramble; free, 24/7. Best at street level after dark, plus an aerial look from Shibuya Sky or the 2nd-floor Starbucks. Shibuya Station.
Shibuya Sky — open-air rooftop over the crossing. 10:00–22:30 (last entry 21:20), ≈¥2,500 online / ¥3,000 at the door; book a sunset slot early. Atop Shibuya Scramble Square. Official info.
Senso-ji — Tokyo's oldest temple; free, grounds open late. Walk Nakamise street for street snacks and souvenirs. Asakusa Station.
Meiji Jingu — a forest shrine in the middle of the city; free, dawn–dusk. Pair with Harajuku (Takeshita St) and Omotesando. Harajuku Station.

teamLab Planets — barefoot, immersive digital-art rooms in Toyosu. ~9:00–22:00 (last entry 21:00), timed 15-min slots, ≈90–150 min inside; book ahead. ⚠ Confirm the 2026 ticket price online. Official site. Shin-Toyosu (Yurikamome).
Shinjuku after dark — Omoide Yokocho (yakitori alley) and Golden Gai (tiny bars); free skyline view from the Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building observation deck. Shinjuku Station.
Akihabara (optional) — anime, games, retro arcades and electronics; a natural fit for your GO/esports crew. Akihabara Station.

title: Where to eat — fast, cheap, good
items: Uobei (Shibuya, Dogenzaka) — sushi fired to your seat on high-speed lanes, plates from ≈¥120, open till ~midnight. Cheap, fast, fun, solo-friendly. (Skip 'Genki Sushi Shibuya' — that original closed in 2020.)
Ichiran — solo ramen booths, order by ticket machine; a Shibuya branch keeps late hours. ⚠ Check the exact branch hours on the day.
Tsukiji Outer Market — sushi & street-food breakfast, 5:00–14:00. Closed Sundays — so this only works if you land early Saturday; otherwise skip it this trip. Tsukiji Station.
Gyudon chains (Yoshinoya, Sukiya, Matsuya) — beef-bowl, often 24 hr, ≈¥500. The reliable cheap-and-fast fallback anywhere.
Konbini (7-Eleven, Lawson, FamilyMart) — genuinely good onigiri, sandos, fried chicken and coffee for a few hundred yen; open 24/7. Don't sleep on it.
Omoide Yokocho (Shinjuku) — smoky yakitori and beer under the tracks; atmospheric and cash-friendly.

title: Carry-on souvenirs & snacks to take home
items: Tokyo Banana — the city's signature banana-custard sponge cake; a solid cake, so it's carry-on-safe. Stations, department-store depachika, and the airport.
Japanese KitKats — matcha, sake, regional flavors; the widest selection is at Don Quijote.
Senbei rice crackers — flat, sturdy, easy to pack; a great cheap gift.
Royce' / matcha chocolate and other depachika sweets — pick a couple; they travel well in a carry-on.
Don Quijote (Donki) — one-stop for snacks, KitKats, skincare and character goods; big Shibuya and Shinjuku branches, open late. Your fastest single-stop souvenir run.

⚠ Carry-on liquid rule: snacks and solid sweets are fine, but anything liquid or gel over 100 ml (sake, yuzu drinks, matcha spreads) must be checked or bought airside past security. Narita has souvenir shops after security if you run short on time.
